title: Pudding and souse
subtitle: Barbadian pickled pork and sweet potato pudding
excerpt: A dense, slightly sweet, and spiced steamed sweet potato pudding, served alongside cold pickled pork souse dressed in lime juice, cucumber, and scotch bonnet. The contrast of the warm, sweet pudding against the cold, sharp souse is the whole point. Pudding and souse is the Saturday lunch dish of Barbados, sold from small shops called pudding and souse shops that open only on Saturdays.
sourceNotes: Pudding and souse is uniquely Barbadian, the combination of steamed sweet potato pudding with pickled pork is not found in quite the same form anywhere else in the Caribbean. The dish appears in Barbadian culinary literature from the 19th century and is associated specifically with Saturday, when pudding and souse shops open for business and queues form before noon. The pickled pork connects to the tradition of preserving pork parts (head, feet, trotters) that was central to Caribbean cooking in the era before refrigeration.

content.text: The souse must be prepared first and chilled while the pudding steams. The pork needs to be boiled until very tender, then cooled completely before the pickling liquid goes on, hot pork in cold pickle becomes mushy. The souse is best served cold from the fridge, creating the temperature contrast with the warm pudding.

Traditional Bajan pudding is made from grated sweet potato, seasoned with spring onion and scotch bonnet, and steamed in a greased bowl. The texture when done is firm enough to slice but soft and moist inside. Do not undercook it, an undercooked pudding is chalky and unpleasant.

Place the {{pork-shoulder}} pieces in a pot, cover with cold water, and bring to a boil. Skim the foam. Simmer for 45 minutes until completely tender. Drain and allow to cool completely. Once cold, combine in a bowl with the lime juice, {{spring-onion}}, {{scotch-bonnet}}, {{black-pepper}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Toss well and refrigerate for at least 1 hour.

Squeeze any excess liquid from the grated {{sweet-potato}}. Combine in a bowl with the {{spring-onion}}, {{scotch-bonnet}}, {{thyme}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Mix well. Grease a heatproof bowl or pudding basin generously with {{unsalted-butter}}. Pack the sweet potato mixture in firmly. Cover tightly with foil. Place in a steamer or set on a trivet in a pot of simmering water, ensuring the water comes halfway up the sides of the bowl. Cover the pot and steam for 45 to 50 minutes until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean. Serve a slice of pudding alongside the cold souse.

items.fix: Sweet potato pudding takes a full 45 minutes over actively simmering water. Check the water level during cooking and top up if needed
items.symptom: Pudding chalky and raw in the centre

items.fix: Use 2 limes instead of 3 and add more only after tasting. The acid softens slightly as the souse chills
Pork shoulder has enough fat to stay moist with 45 minutes of simmering. Longer than this dries it out. Use shoulder, not loin
items.cause: Pork cooked too long or not enough fat

content.text: Pudding and souse is one of the most culturally specific dishes in the Caribbean. Deeply Barbadian. Not found elsewhere in the same form. The Saturday ritual of queuing for pudding and souse at a local shop is a Barbadian institution. The shops are small, family-run, often open from around 10 am until sold out, rarely past early afternoon. In Britain the Barbadian diaspora makes pudding and souse at home for Saturday lunch. The tradition has held for generations, as one of the strongest food connections to Barbados.
